data.title: Modern Warehouses Driving The Next Phase Of Industrial Growth
data.description:
By Art Jones, Senior Director, Global Real Estate Research and Strategy

Despite elevated vacancy rates, the industrial sector remains fundamentally healthy, particularly in modern warehouse facilities. The current softness reflects a cyclical mismatch between supply and leasing velocity, rather than structural weakness. With the construction pipeline contracting and demand for modern logistics assets holding firm, vacancy rates are likely to normalize sooner than expected. As new supply bottoms out by 2026–27, conditions appear favorable for the next development cycle to emerge.

Warehouse net absorption and vacancy by facility age cohort

By facility age, 2020–present

The industrial sector has recently faced headwinds from excess development in select markets and weaker demand tied to policy uncertainty, most notably newly implemented tariffs. These pressures have pushed vacancy rates higher, as new projects came online just as corporate tenants delayed long-term leasing commitments. While this clouds the near-term outlook, it more likely represents temporary cyclical dislocation rather than a sign of structural weakness.

Even so, modern warehouses have remained resilient. Recently built facilities continue to attract strong demand, keeping the sector among the most favored by institutional investors. From 2020 to 2024, warehouses constructed since 2010 averaged 177 million square feet of annual net absorption, well above the long-term average of 103 million. Year-to-date in 2025, this cohort has already absorbed 50 million square feet. Although the current vacancy rate of 15.5% appears elevated, much of it reflects properties still in lease-up rather than stabilized assets underperforming.

Looking ahead, the construction pipeline is expected to keep contracting while demand for modern logistics facilities holds steady. Strong lease-up activity, even in today’s environment, suggests that vacancy will improve more quickly than headlines imply, setting the stage for a more durable recovery. By 2026–27, when new supply is set to reach its lowest point since the Global Financial Crisis, conditions will likely warrant the start of a new development cycle, positioning the sector for its next phase of growth.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Electronic Arts: Sell The Takeover News
data.description:
On Friday, Electronic Arts (EA) went up 15% on news that private equity firm Silver Lake, along with the Saudi Arabia investment fund, is looking to take EA private for as much as $50 billion.

With the news, EA stock leapt to a new all-time high:

EA Stock: Not Much Point In Holding

There is limited upside left in the stock based on what we know now. Assuming a transaction does go through for exactly $50 billion, that would work out to about $199 per share in compensation based on the company's 251 million share count as of last quarter. That's only a few percent potential gain in return for a likely holding period of at least a few months before the transaction closed.

In the event that the deal does not close, there could be considerable downside here. The stock was trading at $170 on Friday morning, for one thing, so that's a much bigger potential decline than the upside if the deal goes through at $199. And earlier this year, EA stock traded as low as $115. Shares had already rallied dramatically prior to the takeover news.

Furthermore, I believe private equity and the Saudi investment fund would be overpaying dramatically if they struck a deal at this price. Here's why I think the deal is likely to produce poor returns for buyers and carries considerable risk for shareholders if the M&A talks fizzle out.

The Video Game Industry Has Changed

The video game has seen a seismic shift in its business model over the past 15 years.

It used to be that big publishers would have a wide catalog of individual games coming out every year. The thinking was similar to movies or music albums. You make a lot of different titles every year. A few will flop, a number will recoup costs or produce a modest profit, and a handful will be blockbusters. Those blockbusters would more than carry the whole operation. Underwrite a lot of unique games across different genres and see what catches on with consumers in any given year.

However, the games industry has increasingly been taken over by finance people and spreadsheet operators rather than gamers or artistic types. This has meant a shift in business focus toward greenlighting projects with a high probability of steady cash flows and predictable returns on investment. Under this thinking, it's better to double down on proven intellectual property and reliable monetization models rather than taking a swing at a new innovation or an out-of-favor game genre.

To EA's credit, this business model pivot worked well over the past decade:

Between 2005 and 2015, EA barely grew its topline at all in inflation-adjusted terms, and the stock price largely didn't go anywhere. Over the past decade, however, where EA has emphasized its leading game franchises rather than investing in a wider content library, it has seen strong results. Revenues nearly doubled between 2015 and 2023. EA stock blasted off.

Since 2023, however, EA's results have come back down to earth. Revenue has stagnated. And the firm's hyper focus on live service model games in particular appears to be starting to run thin.

Earlier this year, fellow Seeking Alpha author Kevin Anthony D. Arroyo wrote a piece on EA titled "How Risk Aversion Is Slowing It Down" which highlighted how companies like EA have largely stopped taking risks on new games and IP in order to protect near-term financial results.

I fully agree with that argument. In my view, EA is increasingly relying on just a few core titles to keep delivering revenue year in and year out rather than creating exciting new games and intellectual property. A certain type of gamer will buy the new EA soccer or football game every year, but these games typically get middling reviews and don't move the needle in terms of advancing the games industry. It's no surprise that Gen Z seems increasingly wary of buying games, given how companies like EA and Activision have shunned making bold new properties in terms of focusing on bland but dependable revenue-generators.

EA Is Now A Live Services And Subscription Model Business

In the latest 12 months of results, shown below, EA generated $5.4 billion in live services revenues and just $2.0 billion from full game sales:

Many investors probably think of games as a transaction where you'd go to Best Buy (BBY) or GameStop (GME), plunk down $50, and pick up the latest and greatest new game. And that was the end of the transaction.

But that sort of game sale (and the equivalent through digital stores) is now a modest fraction of revenues for big publishers like EA. Rather, the big bucks are in selling monthly subscriptions, game passes, and potentially addictive in-game monetization methods such as loot boxes.

It's well-trodden territory to critique EA for how it manages its sports franchises. And sports are something of a unique niche within gaming. So, I'll focus my attention elsewhere.

The Sims 4: A Case Study

I'd highlight another EA property, The Sims 4, for a great example of how EA's lack of innovation is currently driving robust profits but could end up leading to failure over the intermediate-term.

The original Sims and Sims 2 were pioneers of the life simulation genre and are widely-studied among gaming historians. The developer, Maxis, was a highly respected creative shop that launched all sorts of quirky games like SimTower and SimGolf that have long since turned purely into nostalgia fodder and hold no commercial relevance today.

However, Maxis also had its consistently successful SimCity series, which was highly-popular up through SimCity 4.

Under EA's leadership, however, the company decided to make the fifth mainline SimCity game an online-only experience; turning the classic city builder into a live service game with enhanced monetization opportunities that could theoretically deliver strong financial upside. The problem is that city builders tend to be single-player games, and the fan base revolted against the online bells and whistles. The fifth SimCity game was a colossal flop, and EA exited the city-building genre after that. The ground was entirely ceded to Paradox Interactive (OTCPK:PRXXF), which would go on to sell more than 12 million copies of its rival Cities Skylines in the city-building genre that EA had left behind.

City builders aren't that large of a genre, so EA giving up on it isn't the end of the world from a financial perspective.

That said, this could be a bad omen for the Sims as well. That's because EA has seemingly abandoned innovation there as well. The Sims 4 originally came out in 2014, meaning it is more than 11 years old at this point. In August of this year, EA suggested there will not be a Sims 5 anytime soon.

Sims 4, for its part, tends to be buggy and can run poorly if you install a significant number of its seemingly unceasing number of downloaded content packs and other in-game purchasable items.

When I say unceasing, I'm not kidding. As per online game shop Steam, there are now 102 pieces of distinct downloadable content for Sims 4. The overall cost? Well, I'll let you see for yourself:

All Sims 4 available DLC, United States Steam store, as of Sept. 26th, 2025.

It's truly remarkable to think that to get the full experience, it would now cost more than $1,500. Who would pay that much?

The thing is, EA has been releasing new DLCs for this game for 11 years now. So, fans pay $40 here, $20 here, $15 there, and it adds up.

But it becomes intimidating for new players. Who is going to start out as a new player of an 11-year-old game where you'd need to spend a thousand bucks or more to get the full experience? This infinite DLC model for an increasingly outdated game has generated a ton of cash, sure, but it is irritating fans and leaving EA vulnerable to competition from new life sims, just as Paradox dethroned EA as the king of city builder simulations.

To that point, South Korean firm Krafton launched its own life sim game, InZOI, earlier this year and sold more than one million copies in a single week. While InZOI has some issues of its own, it is a fresh, great-looking game competing against an 11-year-old peer. As long as EA seems to think it has a monopoly on the genre and thus underinvests in The Sims franchise, it is ripe to be disrupted. In addition to InZOI, another promising life sim competitor, Paralives, is expected to release in December of this year.

Private equity may see a long, steady string of Sims 4 revenues historically and think these will continue in perpetuity. But the game is more than a decade-old and competition is arriving rather quickly now. As long as EA refuses to invest the tens (perhaps hundreds) of millions of dollars necessary to make a proper sequel, it is leaving the door wide open for a new rival to come in and siphon off much of the player base with a fresh, modern spin on the genre. Just as EA lost SimCity to new and improved competition, I believe it will lose the Sims within the next 5-7 years as well.

EA Needs To Invest More To Maintain Its Moat

I play several new games every month and follow the industry closely. I could go on all day about EA's strategic decisions and how they've garnered substantial consumer backlash across many of their gaming IPs.

I'll sum up, though, and say that after a decade of focusing on juicing profitability and cutting costs, I believe EA is near the maximum possible returns that it could obtain with the current business model.

Franchises like Sims 4 are seemingly worn out and tired at this point. EA risks losing players both to newer and more compelling games and also other alternative entertainment options such as streaming video or social media. We already see that with Gen Z, who is reportedly spending much less on gaming than Millennials do. And Gen Z is more prone to playing one or two games religiously rather than sampling and buying a wider array of different titles. With properties like Fortnite and Minecraft soaking up tons of attention, it leaves less room for EA's games, especially among younger users.

Put simply, it's great that EA has its games that are huge with millennials. But it remains to be seen if it will be able to make similar inroads with Gen Z. And if you're private equity making a long-term bet on EA's portfolio, that's a key question that has to be addressed.

Valuation Seems Rich

I'm concerned about this deal's valuation. Analysts see the company generating adjusted earnings per share of $8.28 in fiscal year 2026. At a $50 billion valuation, this would put EA stock around 24 times adjusted earnings. Not terrible, right?

However, I don't think those adjusted earnings are the best way to look at the company. There's a huge amount of stock compensation here that is getting backed out of those adjusted earnings.

Excluding a one-time tax benefit in 2020, which distorted the numbers, EA's actual GAAP earnings have been pretty consistently between $3 and $4 per share for the past decade:

At a takeout price of $199, representative of a $50 billion valuation, a buyer would be paying close to 50x GAAP earnings. What's going on here? In the most recent quarter, EA reported $450 million in non-GAAP operating income. But it spent $152 million -- more than a third of that -- on stock-based compensation. Throw in some acquisition costs, and actual EPS came in far short of the adjusted figure.

I would guess that a sizable chunk of this stock-based comp will turn into cash salary expenses going forward. If EA doesn't want its skilled developers and senior employees to leave, the stock comp will need to be made up for in some other way to retain talent.

We can debate what portion of the adjustments to earnings makes sense here or not. Regardless, however, I see the 24x adjusted earnings figure as being optimistic and would encourage investors to closely consider EA's historical GAAP earnings when thinking about what this business might be worth in an M&A deal.

EA Stock Bottom Line

The gaming industry is in a challenging position right now. There was a huge downturn in 2023 and 2024. We saw the largest layoffs in the industry's history. And some industry experts I spoke with compared the recent downturn to the Atari crash of 1983, giving a sense of just how unprecedented the recent issues have been.

It remains to be seen how the big games companies will react. Firms like Ubisoft Entertainment (OTCPK:UBSFY) have had a brutal go of it trying to adapt to the times:

Admittedly, Ubisoft has made some uniquely questionable strategic decisions in recent years. Still, I am skeptical that EA's business is worthy of an all-time high valuation, as other gaming companies are on the rocks. And EA, for its part, has seen its growth slow to a crawl as well.

I think EA has already picked most of the low-hanging fruit from the live service model. Counterpart Sony (SONY) has cancelled eight of its 12 live service games that were in development, as the live services market is saturated. I think there are limits to EA's current game plan if it doesn't invest in more top-notch single-player games.

The big publishers, including EA, are at risk of ceding market share to AA and smaller independent studios that still take innovative risks and build games on more modest budgets. There has been a ton of talent fired from the top studios since 2023, meaning that smaller studios can grab world-class developers. We're also seeing increasingly high-quality games debut from studios in countries like China, South Korea, and Poland, which have lower development costs than the United States.

In addition, the rise of top-notch game engines like Unity (U) and Unreal has lowered the barrier for entry in creating new games. Presumably, artificial intelligence will further lower the cost of game development going forward, opening the door to additional competition.

EA is in a position to be disrupted, and at the valuation private equity is offering to pay here, any disruption could make this deal look like a strategic misstep.

It's hard to get a real sense of what sorts of cash flow EA's IPs can generate over the long term. You can guess what The Sims will generate next year or in 2027, sure. But if EA refuses to invest in making a Sims 5, the cash flows from the franchise are likely to collapse at some point in the not-all-that-distant future. The sports games are probably the safest bet for long-term revenue generation, but even there, some of the more aggressive monetization efforts like loot boxes could be curtailed by regulators going forward.

Long story short, EA generated $1.04 billion in net income over the past 12 months. EA would have to run as-is for close to 50 years for the PE buyer to get back their initial $50 billion outlay at that price. And given that EA's revenues have now stagnated and GAAP EPS has only modestly moved up over the past decade, I wouldn't count on growth making up for this lofty transaction multiple either. If anything, as Millennials get older and Gen Z moves into the prime gaming demographic, EA's profits may decline if the company can't improve its connection to younger gamers.

EA has already trimmed its overhead costs and reduced risk while pushing monetization techniques aggressively. It's unclear to me what levers private equity could pull to improve profitability if and when they take control of EA. This deal just doesn't make a lot of sense to me intuitively.

And as for EA stock owners today, I see very little reason to stay in the game. I'd be highly surprised if anyone bid higher than $50 billion.

If the $50 billion deal closes, that is only a few percent upside from here. And if the deal falls through, I would expect shares to trade back to the $160s if not lower.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Breaking Down IEA's Oil Supply Report: Key Insights For Energy Investors
data.description:
Have you noticed the price at the pump lately? Those numbers reflect a complex global oil market undergoing significant transformation. The International Energy Agency’s latest report reveals a fundamental shift in supply/demand dynamics that every energy investor should understand.

Global oil production has reached unprecedented levels, creating a widening gap between supply and demand. This imbalance continues to expand as production growth significantly outpaces modest consumption increases. The result is expanding inventories that place persistent downward pressure on prices, creating challenging conditions for producers but potential opportunities for consumers.

The geographic source of this production surge tells an equally important story. Non-OPEC+ countries, particularly those in the Americas, drive most expansion. The United States, Brazil, Canada and Guyana collectively contribute the majority of global production growth. This regional concentration represents a fundamental power shift in global energy markets, reducing OPEC’s traditional pricing influence and market control.

Meanwhile, demand growth remains tepid compared to surging supply. Interestingly, forecasters disagree significantly about future consumption trends. The IEA projects modest demand growth, while OPEC maintains a more optimistic outlook. This forecaster divergence creates additional market uncertainty, complicating investment decisions across the energy sector.

Perhaps most concerning for producers, global oil inventories continue their steady climb. The IEA warns that without intervention, stocks will build at unsustainable rates through year-end. These inventory builds typically precede periods of price weakness, creating potential headwinds for energy-focused investments dependent on stronger price environments.

For energy investors, these dynamics require careful consideration. The persistent supply/demand imbalance suggests continued price pressure through 2025 and beyond. Companies with lower production costs, reduced debt levels and operational flexibility may navigate these challenges more successfully than highly leveraged producers dependent on higher prices.

The current environment rewards selectivity rather than broad sector exposure. Understanding these fundamental shifts in market dynamics helps investors position appropriately for the evolving energy landscape where traditional assumptions about market balance and price support may no longer apply.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. We have not sought, nor have we received, permission from any third-party to include their information in this article. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements. The information contained herein represents our subjective belief and opinions and should not be construed as investment, tax, legal, or financial advice.

Original Source: Infrastructure Capital Advisors

data.title: OXLC: You Were Warned
data.description:
Today we return to look at the super high yielding and often misunderstood Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:OXLC). On July 1st of this year, we issued a sell rating. That was at a pre reverse split price of about $4.20, or $21 now post-reverse split. Here we are at just over $17. Oddly, that is a bit of a discount, but two things have happened.

First, the reverse split. Our policy has always been to avoid stocks (or in this case a CEF) that has the need to reverse split. Almost always the price continues to wither. Second, the distribution has been cut again. In short, while you can catch waves where the value goes up, over time, you are sacrificing principal for income. Meanwhile, the managers are charging high fees for managing the funds. It is hard to get ahead, but if you are in it long enough, you can of course recoup all of your principal back through the distribution, and then anything after that would be considered gains. That is of course if you are taking the cash directly, and not trying to compound.

We can see the appeal, but, all of these super high income machines tend to see the same trend of eroding NAV and loss of principal over time. At today's prices, we are neutral, rather than an outright sell. In the near-term we see this moving more sideways, which is good for income. But longer-term, we still see it as a clear risk to your principal. Please be careful, as we have warned you before, and now again.

Pretty massive swing here and on par with pandemic level pricing now. As you can see the decline picked up steam in 2022 and 2023 when the Fed began its rate-hiking campaign. When rates stabilized, the name traded within a tight range which for a high-income name is of course a positive. That is kind of what we expect the next few months, but over time still see it eroding lower. Gone are the days of zero rates. This fund does best with stable borrowing rates, and is of course a bit rate sensitive. That is why we saw the big fall in 2022. Now rates are being cut, but the bond market has been moving up and down. Sharp moves tend to be a negative here.

Remember How Oxford Lane Capital Corporation Operates

This is a closed-end fund, or a CEF. That in and of itself does not mean the investment is good or bad. A CEF is simply a type of investment that raises a fixed amount of capital through its IPO and then invests that capital in a portfolio of assets. CEFs do not continuously issue and redeem shares but do raise money. Make no mistake OXLC has raised many billions of dollars to invest. Much of the demand for this CEF is related to pricing relative to the fund's net asset value, or NAV. Right now, we are trading right at or slightly below NAV, which is when we would lean more toward the investment being a near term buy and/or hold. NAV can move higher of course if the value of the investments increase, but there are a number of reasons NAV has eroded over time which we will touch on. But you need to understand how it operates.

OXLC invests in collateralized loan obligations, or CLO, equity positions. Ok great. The average investor does not know what these are generally speaking. CLOs are pretty complex instruments and or new investors the can be hard understand. We will try to simplify it. We did explain this in a bit more detail in the article we linked in the opening. So we won't go to far into detail, but essentially a CLO is a group of loans that are bundled together into a single security and sold as a single investment. Starts to get hairy the deeper you go into these and what is in each CLO. A lot of these products were involved in contributing to the Great Recession, especially since CLOs consisting of many mortgages of varying quality were packaged together. And money is being made up and down the chain. A CLO purchases bunches of bank loans and then sells off some of the cash flow. Each time it sells a portion of the cash flow, or a tranche if you will, the CLO's organizers charge a slight premium to investors, and make some money off of it. Oh, and then of course the tranches are also ranked, the first set gets paid first, but often with lower interest. As we noted in the article linked in the opening, it:

"kind of operates like senior debt, if you will. Moving down the line becomes more risky, so-called junior debt positions, but this comes often with higher interest than the senior tranches. Because there is cash flow in these tranches, CLOs attract a lot of investment. And this is where OXLC puts its money to work.

Looking to history, it is hard to argue the approach works long-term, but here we are. Movements in rates and general credit risk impacts the CLOs value, and with OXLC, can impact its NAV.

Net Asset Value Eroding

So why are there massive drops in OXLC? It is because of the drops in NAV. And NAV falls because of the risk of these investments, primarily, which is credit risk. And the other part of this has to do with the aforementioned rank of the CLO investment. Depending on which tranche order has been invested in, remember being in line from senior to juniors, this determines when the investors are paid interest. Last in line? Well then you are paid interest after all the other tranches are paid. And if you are invested in the equity and a borrower defaults, it is the equity tranche that gets less. The lower you go, the higher the risk. And when rates are high, and on the move wildly, it leads to varying values of the CLOs and varying risk of default which puts pressure on shares. In theory lower rates makes credit easier to access and lower the risk of default, which we can see to a degree in the chart out of the pandemic. Although rates are being cut slowly, we are not headed back to zero or next to zero borrowing rates.

So, if you buy OXLC, you are buying exposure to CLOs, and all the benefits and risks that come with them. You can almost think of it as if you are buying 'call options' on the CLOs. It is not the best comparison, but helps investors better understand what they are getting exposure to. Since OXLC invests in CLOs and the cash flow tranches, we consider it a levered play on the CLOs. And as such, the prices of the loans and CLOs will impact the NAV of OXLC.

When CLO prices do go up, OXLC's NAV should of course typically rise in turn, but if prices are falling, the downside to OXLC's NAV is often greater. You see, the leveraged nature of OXLC to CLO equity values results in large moves to the CEF's value, similar to owning options against a common stock or ETF. And though there is a higher level rate sensitivity, the prices of CLOs themselves do not actually move all that much with interest rates. Also it is worth reminding you that generally speaking CLOs are floating rate, and that means that the yields and/or the cash flow the generate, can move with them.

Although there have been dividend cuts over the years, there are still times when the dividend cannot be fully covered, and that leads to NAV erosion over time. Why is that? As a credit investment fund we generally expect long-term to see NAV go down over time. If there are returns of capital, that comes out of NAV. Is it possible to have made money here? Sure, at certain entry points and time frames, absolutely. But we stand by our assertion that for the most part, in exchange for your principal, you have generated cash flow. While there are exceptions and we are sure we will hear about it from those who are bullish here, this tends to be the trend. Everyone's financial situation is different, and some investors may be ok with trading off principal for income, but our mantra is to protect your principal.

Final Thoughts

Look this is a super high-yield income machine, but over time, outside of select entry points, or being a very early investor that has held on, you are losing your principal in exchange for dividends/income. Big moves in rates can lead to varying CLO pricing. Credit risk is real. While the ten-year total return is positive, it trails many instruments. This is a pretty high fee fund for the managers to do their work of generating income by investing in CLO equity tranches for cash flow. Net asset value has eroded over time, a common theme for many credit investment funds. Right now you can buy at NAV or even slightly below, which is the best time if you are going to buy. We do expect some sideways action ahead which near-term is good for OXLC investors. Long-term, more NAV erosion is all but guaranteed. At the end of the day, it is your money, and while OXLC is not for us, if you are ok with the risk to principal for income, then it might be for you.

data.title: Alphabet: The Universal Edge
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My earlier bullish call on Alphabet/Google (NASDAQ:GOOGL), (NASDAQ:GOOG), has aged well. Since late April, the stock has climbed around 62%, impressively outperforming the wider U.S. market. That’s a big win for anyone who bought shares earlier this year. I’m still very optimistic about Google, and here’s why: the management’s execution is exceptionally effective and is rapidly expanding the company’s presence in AI. Google is busy building a powerful AI ecosystem that is gaining strength by the day, bolstering the company as a leading player in next-generation technology. I’m also encouraged by the steady progress of other growth factors, such as the autonomous-taxi business Waymo, which could someday transform urban transportation, as well as the company’s expansion into the Smart TV arena. All these are signs that Google is thinking outside the box in terms of new growth opportunities. Add in a strong balance sheet and excellent quarterly results, and this giant is worth long-term attention. And yet the stock’s valuation remains reasonable relative to some insane modern-world valuation. All in all, I am reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating for GOOGL.

Recent Developments

Google’s recent quarterly results were excellent, especially for a company already worth over $3 trillion. The company didn’t just meet Wall Street expectations, it beat them on both revenue and profit. Revenue jumped 13.8% YoY, which is impressive growth for such a giant. More importantly, this growth came from a balanced mix of businesses; Search, YouTube, Cloud, and Other Bets all contributed.

Profits grew even faster; non-GAAP EPS climbed from $1.89 to $2.31 year over year. What’s even more impressive is the quality of this growth, because it was fed by solid operational leverage. The company’s gross margin also expanded from 58.1% to 59.5%, giving the management plenty of room to invest in R&D. In fact, R&D rose by $2 billion YoY, showing that Google is seriously investing in AI and cloud computing while still managing to stay profitable.

Such strong profits keep the balance sheet healthy and solid. As we can see, Google boasts a comfortable cash pile of more than twice the size of its total debt, giving the company ample flexibility to fund its growth projects, make acquisitions, and buy back shares—without relying on borrowing.

While the above numbers are impressive, there is a list of other factors I find particularly bullish and which underpin my rating. Firstly, I am impressed by the company’s rapid progress in AI and cloud services. Google’s Gemini AI model recently overtook OpenAI’s ChatGPT in downloads, hitting its $3 trillion market cap. This is key because it eases investor fears that AI might threaten Google’s core search business. In fact, Gemini’s popularity shows that Google is thriving even as AI reshapes how humans search for information. The development led to Google Cloud reporting impressive growth with $106 billion in remaining obligations.

Next is the fact that investment firm TD Cowen raised its price target for Google shares from $240 to $270 based on a survey that showed that 44% of companies adding a second cloud provider now choose Google. This figure is up from 37% last year. As mentioned, Google’s Gemini platform is also gaining ground, with 54% of survey respondents now using it. This makes Gemini the second most popular large-scale language model. These figures shows that Google is clearly holding its own even when it trails behind giants like AWS and Azure.

Another bullish development is Wedbush’s recently released opinion regarding the $100-billion Nvidia-OpenAI agreement to build AI infrastructure. Now, while this doesn’t directly involve Google, the sheer scale of this global AI buildout will require more data centers, and exactly the kind of projects that boost the need for Google Cloud services. In short, this is a deal that spells more demand for Google.

As I mentioned before, Google is expanding its Gemini beyond phones and computers—into television. The new Google TV is designed to take the guesswork out of what to watch by providing personalized AI recommendations. This means that viewers can literally engage in conversation with their TV, telling it exactly what they want to watch with the familiar “Hey Google” command. I view this as a smart move: not only is this bound to boost user engagement, it will also build on Google’s data collection abilities and offer the company more advertising opportunities.

Last but not least, I wanted to touch on Google’s Waymo autonomous taxi business. Waymo operates self-driving taxis in large cities like Phoenix and San Francisco, and is currently testing new markets. In addition, the company recently introduced "Waymo for Business", which means expansion of the autonomous taxi service to the B2B segment. Although autonomous taxis are still in the early stages of development, the potential is massive. Just imagine if Waymo scaled its services to major cities across the world? It would become an additional multibillion-dollar revenue stream for Google, one that’s completely separate from its cloud and ad operations.

Valuation Among The Risks

Now for the cautionary red flags. As we can see below, according to Wall Street consensus, the Google share price, currently around $245, is slightly above the average analyst target price of about $236.82. This suggests the stock might be a little overvalued in the short term.

But here I believe we need to look at the valuation context. Google’s P/E ratio is roughly in line with historical averages, meaning investors are paying similar multiples as in the past, despite the company’s many growth drivers. Meanwhile, the P/S is slightly elevated. Even so, analysts are expecting earnings to grow quickly enough to cause P/S compression over time, meaning that profits could rise faster than sales. This would allow the company to better fit its valuation, and make the stock look cheaper.

Competition is, in my view, probably the biggest risk factor. Rivals like Microsoft and Amazon make pretty fierce opponents in both cloud computing and AI. They are giants in this field and are actively investing billions of dollars in AI infrastructure. It is essential for Google to keep innovating if it’s to stay competitive in this market.

Another concern worth considering is regulatory pressure. Antitrust scrutiny is a constant risk simmering in the background. In fact, regulators both in the U.S. and abroad have explored breaking up parts of Google’s business just to reduce its market dominance. I wouldn’t say this is a high-probability risk, just one to bear in mind.

While these risks are very real, they do not, in my opinion, outweigh the solid long-term opportunity provided by Google’s strong position in AI and cloud computing.

Bottom Line

To conclude, GOOGL and GOOG are still "strong buys." Google continues to deliver excellent results thanks to its AI innovation, strong cloud growth, and fastidious financial management. It is also expanding its existing ecosystem at a staggering rate. Add in a solid balance sheet and a hefty cash pillow complete with rising margins, and this is a company poised to take advantage of the opportunities in a rapidly growing market—in spite of the competition. Yes, there are risks, but to me, they are relatively small, especially when compared with the company’s growth drivers, not to mention its solid history and reasonable valuation.

data.title: Arm Holdings: Fantastic Growth, But Too Pricey To Chase
data.description:
Arm Holdings plc (NASDAQ:ARM) is a semiconductor company that designs and licenses the ARM architecture for central processing units (CPUs) and other chips. The company doesn't manufacture chips itself but offers software-based designs and tools for ARM infrastructure and provides them as intellectual property.

The company experiences significant AI-driven demand due to its energy-efficient processor cores, which are used in a wide range of artificial intelligence applications, including autonomous vehicles, generative AI, and data centers. Given that, the stock price has tripled over the course of the last 3 years, but has underperformed the broader market over the past 12 months, implying a negative 4% return.

ARM's valuation appears to be inflated, as the company trades at a forward P/E of 85. I think the semiconductor is a phenomenal business, a fantastic top and bottom-line growth story, but I think it is likely to consolidate further until valuations come down to more compelling levels.

I think that Arm Holdings is a Hold, with a $150 price target over the next 12 months, representing 7% upside potential, and the broader market underperformance. ARM is a strong company, but a significant premium implies that the market knows it; therefore, I believe further multiple expansion may be limited in the short term.

FQ1 2026: The First Ever Top-Line Miss, Followed By 13% Selloff

On July 30, the company delivered FQ1 2026 results. This was a mixed quarter, as double-digit top-line growth was clouded by an $8 million revenue miss (0.8% surprise), followed by a 13% selloff over the following trading day. This was the first time ever a top-line miss, since the company was listed on the NASDAQ in 2023.

Arm Holdings reported $1.05 billion in revenue, reflecting roughly 12% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $0.35 in diluted EPS, nearly 18% decline versus $0.40 EPS the same quarter last year, but in line with market expectations.

On the upside, management underscored a massive, 25% year-over-year growth for royalty revenue. It has reached $585 million in the quarter, mainly driven by continued adoption of its architecture and Arm-based chips in data centers. This indicates a high-quality product and significant demand. If AI-driven demand continues, ARM appears to be well-positioned to benefit from it.

I think the following slide from the company's presentation greatly reflects the strong position ARM has in the chip market. The semiconductor operates in the high-growth, high-demand markets, with expectations of continued growth. Arm is a well-diversified company, operating in different segments, from mobile applications to cloud computing and the internet of things, which reduces risk and enables a strong, positive future outlook.

This also acts as a substantial growth tailwind, as if management is able to sustain its market share, based on the projected market growth, it should be able to significantly benefit from expansion via royalties.

Management has also guided for the following quarter. The company anticipates revenue to come in at around $1.06 billion at the midpoint, implying roughly 19% revenue growth on a year-over-year basis. ARM also expects diluted EPS to come in at about $0.33 for the quarter, up by 10% versus the same quarter last year.

Arm Holdings anticipates strong demand in the future. Given AI-related tailwinds and other companies such as Micron Technology and Marvell Technology delivering strong quarterly results, supporting significant demand for AI-related products, I think the trend will continue. ARM appears to be well-positioned to continue its top and bottom line growth at a double-digit pace.

Arm Holdings Is A High-Quality Business, With A Strong Moat, But Trades At A Significant Premium

The company trades at a forward P/E of about 85, which represents roughly 3.3x higher valuation compared to the industry's average of 25. ARM is also 3.5x more expensive than the average company in the S&P 500 at almost a 24x earnings multiple. This semiconductor is a phenomenal top and bottom line growth story, but at such a premium, it may need to unlock another growth catalyst for multiple expansion.

The forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 3.57, roughly 2x higher than the sector median of 1.83, supports my bearish narrative, which indicates significant overvaluation and that upside may be limited. The chart above reveals that over the past several years, Arm Holdings was trading at elevated levels, but it has also consolidated since then. I think this trend may continue further.

The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales at 31.69 and 32.22, respectively, highlight a 9x higher valuation versus peers' average of 3.52 for both metrics. This suggests that the market may have already priced in the future growth of the company, and at such a premium, even with continued strong performance from management, there is a risk of further multiple consolidation. It seems that the company valuation has run ahead of its fundamentals by at least three years, which may imply that any uncertainties around its business could be met with a correction in the stock price.

On the upside, ARM is a low-leverage company with total obligations of $396 million. It has about $2.91 billion in cash; therefore, the company could pay its debt with ease. This leaves room for financial flexibility, if needed, and the company appears to be well-positioned in a high-interest-rate environment. Last quarter, ARM Holdings generated $27 million in interest and investment income.

The company grew its revenue by almost 18% over the past 12 months, and that partially offsets the bearish narrative. This is roughly 2.6x higher than the industry's average of about 7%, and an 80% premium versus the average company in the benchmark, with a 10% top-line growth. This supports the thesis that ARM should be trading at a premium, but I am not sure whether it justifies more than 3x higher valuations.

ARM Holdings grew EBITDA by 217% over the past year, implying a massive, 21x higher rate versus the industry's average of roughly 10%. EBIT grew by 417% on a year-over-year basis, representing nearly 34x outperformance compared to the sector median of 12%. This supports a bullish stance for the company, and if management continues to deliver such incredible growth, the semiconductor may continue trading at elevated levels. ARM grew its bottom line by 64% over the past year, almost 5.8x outperformance versus peers of 11%, justifying the company trading at a premium.

ARM Holdings operates at a significantly higher than market-like gross profit margin (TTM) of 97%, implying a 96% difference from peers of 49%. The net income margin (TTM) of approximately 17% is exceptional, and indicates nearly 4x outperformance compared to the industry's average of 4%. These are incredible results, which support my thesis of ARM being a highly profitable company. This also justifies the semiconductor trading at a premium to both peers and the benchmark.

The company delivered an 11% return on common equity (TTM), 104% higher than the sector median of 5.39%. ARM generated $1.02 billion in cash from operations (TTM), which indicates an 8x advantage versus peers of only $120 million.

Overall, ARM Holdings appears to be a high-quality semiconductor company that operates at significantly higher than market margins. It is also a highly profitable business, which is growing its top and bottom line at a double-digit pace. On the downside, the company trades at a significant premium to both peers and the benchmark, which implies that the market knows what a phenomenal company ARM is. This could limit upside potential in the short term, even if management continues to deliver such an exceptional growth pace.

Wall Street forecasts $1.69 in EPS for FY2026, which would imply a 3% increase on a year-over-year basis. I think this is a rational estimate, which also limits the positive outlook and multiple expansion in the short term. Given the strong moat and growth tailwinds tied to ARM's business, I think this semiconductor is well-positioned to benefit from market expansion, but because of already elevated trading levels, it may consolidate further.

If we apply an 88x earnings multiple for FY2026 EPS of $1.69, we come in at my price target of $150 over the next year. This implies 7% upside. This would also result in the company trading at a significant premium for longer, but I think it may be justified with phenomenal business, above-market margins, and a low-leverage capital structure. I think the company may need a new growth catalyst to unlock multiple expansion, but given its phenomenal results, ARM may sustain its current earnings multiple. On the other hand, this multiple's elevation reflects about a 3.5x premium to both peers and the benchmark, which may cap the upside in the near term.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Arm Holdings that may affect the stock price in the short term.

The company trades at a significant premium, which may imply there is little room for error. If the AI-related demand slows down, the market could correct, which could substantially take ARM alongside other AI names. To this, despite the company anticipating strong future growth, if the outlook changes, sentiment could shift dramatically, which could result in multiple contractions.

ARM faces fierce competition. Open-source architectures are gaining more attention, which poses a long-term threat to the company's licensing business, out of which the semiconductor earns royalties. This could materially hurt top-line growth, and the stock's valuations may not be justifiable at elevated levels.

The company is well-diversified, but part of its business is highly sensitive to macroeconomic concerns. A portion of the company's royalties revenue comes from consumer markets and smartphones. If trade tension emerges or new tariff concerns appear, this could tremendously impact consumer sentiment, which may pull demand.

On the other hand, if none of the above materializes, the company could appreciate in price alongside its bottom-line growth. I think ARM may be a compelling long-term play for investors who seek AI exposure, but at current elevations, it is expensive and leaves a little room for error.

Conclusion: High-Quality AI Name With Strong Moat, But Not Sure Whether Growth Justifies A Premium

Arm Holdings appears to be a high-quality name, which grows revenue in double digits, and offers a high-demand ARM infrastructure to consumers. The company operates at significantly above-market margins, appears to be highly profitable, with multiple growth tailwinds ahead. ARM is also a low-leverage semiconductor, which benefits from a high-interest environment. The company earns a significant amount in interest and investment income, which supports bottom-line growth.

On the other hand, its valuations appear to be elevated, with above 3x higher earnings multiple versus peers and the benchmark. Even if management continues to deliver significant results, it may not translate to multiple expansions. I think ARM may need to unlock a new growth cycle for multiple expansions. Until then, I believe it could consolidate further.

This is why I think that Arm Holdings is a Hold, with a $150 price target over the next year. This reflects 7% upside potential, but indicates broader market underperformance. I think ARM appears to be a strong AI-related name, but I believe upside potential could be limited, given its already inflated multiple.

39 Wall Street analysts forecast a roughly $153 12-month price target, indicating nearly 9% upside potential. This comes in line with my guidance. The company is phenomenal, but it seems that the market already knows it.

data.title: The Bancorp: Time For A Downgrade After A Great Run Higher
data.description:
Over the last few years now, I have really gotten into analyzing financial firms. One enterprise that I have established a pretty good track record with this year is The Bancorp (NASDAQ:TBBK). Since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate in June of this year, citing its strong asset quality and low uninsured deposit exposure, the stock has risen by 37.1%. That's massively higher than the 8.1% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. And since I last upgraded it from a ‘hold’ to a ‘buy’ in March of this year, shares are up 50.3%. That's well above the 21.1% increase that the broader market enjoyed.

At some point, even the best companies deserve a downgrade. And sadly, I think that time has finally come. Operationally speaking, The Bancorp is still doing very well in some respects. But there are certain metrics that are continuing to decline. In addition to this, the company is not as cheap as it was previously. And I don't mean that from a share price perspective. Rather, I mean it from a valuation standpoint. Although asset quality remains strong, that only takes a firm so far. Because of all of this, I think that rating it a ‘hold’ as opposed to the ‘buy’ I have had it at for most of this year is the right choice.

Time to take a step back

As I have detailed in the past, The Bancorp is not your typical bank. The company prides itself in its unique business model sees it serve as a fintech solutions provider that caters to specialized markets. Since I have covered this in previous articles, I won't really dig deep into it. But in short, the company helps to facilitate payments and lending activities in key markets. It offers institutional banking services, commercial lending services, and even real estate bridge lending services. It partners with program managers like PayPal (PYPL) and Chime, as well as processors, payment networks, and regulators.

Recently, however, financial performance has been rather mixed. For the most recent quarter, which would be the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the company booked net interest income of $53.1 million. That's down from the $92.5 million that the company reported one year earlier. This drop in net interest income was driven almost entirely by an $43.2 million provision for credit losses on consumer financial technology and loans that the business offers. And for the first half of this year, the $89.1 million in provision for credit losses pushed net interest income down to $98 million compared to the $184.8 million reported the previous year.

As disastrous as this looks at first glance, it is worth noting that the firm enters into arrangements with its financial technology partners whereby they will essentially reimburse it for credit quality issues. That's why non-interest income in the most recent quarter surged to $83.7 million compared to the $30.7 million reported the previous year. And for the first half of 2025, the $167.4 million that the company saw was massively above the $60.1 million reported the previous year. In terms of profitability, we have seen results that are much more stable. Net income of $59.8 million in the second quarter of this year came in above the $53.7 million reported one year earlier. And for the first half of this year, net profits totaled $117 million compared to the $110.1 million reported previously.

When it comes to the balance sheet, we have seen some pain as of late. In particular, deposits in the most recent quarter amounted to $7.77 billion. That represents a meaningful drop from the $8.36 billion reported for the first quarter of this year and was just a hair above the $7.75 billion that the company reported at the end of last year. The decline seen from the first quarter to the second quarter was likely attributable to multiple factors, including a competitive interest rate environment and seasonal fluctuations. But it is something that investors should be paying attention to. However, with only $560.1 million, or 7.2%, of the company’s overall deposit base classified as uninsured, I would say that there is very little risk of a meaningful downturn in deposits moving forward.

From the end of last year to the second quarter of this year, the loans that the company has on its books grew from $6.08 billion to $6.48 billion. That is great to see in and of itself. It is important to note that the greatest exposure that the company has to loans would be the $2.14 billion dedicated to real estate bridge loans. But it also has a lot of exposure to SBLOC/IBLOC loans that stand at $1.60 billion. For context, these are loans that are collateralized by marketable securities or by the cash value of life insurance policies.

In the most recent quarter, securities for the institution amounted to $1.48 billion. That was down slightly from the $1.50 billion reported for the final quarter of last year. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents went from $570.1 million to $340.3 million. But as disappointing as that was to see, I don't really see the issue when you consider the growth in loans that the institution enjoyed. At the same time, debt has remained pretty much stable, totaling $123.7 million as of the end of the second quarter.

By continuing to leverage its growth opportunities, particularly in the financial technology space, the company has had high expectations for the future. When I last wrote about the company, management forecasted that earnings per share this year would be $5.25. But now, we have updated estimates for the future. Why all management still expects earnings per share to hit $5.25 this year, they expect further growth to $7 on an annualized basis by the final quarter of next year. That represents significant growth and will come not only from the new partnerships, products, and services, that the company intends to engage in, but also by making different structural changes and buying back stock. In fact, the company has planned a $200 million senior notes issuance in the third quarter of this year that should help to facilitate buybacks.

If we use those estimates for 2025 and 2027, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently valued. For the 2025 figures, we are looking at a price to earnings multiple of 14.7. And using the 2026 estimates, keeping in mind that it's annualized for the final quarter of that year, so really it would mean more a reading of 2027, we would get a price to earnings multiple of 11. I typically like to see a price to earnings multiple in this space of 10 or lower. It is important to note that, as that same chart illustrates, three of the five firms that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is on this basis. In the chart below, I then looked at the company through the lens of the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In both of these instances, the business was well above the competition, with all five of the enterprises I compared it to being meaningfully cheaper than it is.

In the past, I was open to these lofty multiples because of how high the asset quality of the institution is. However, there are limits to this. Shares are quite a bit more expensive than they were when I last wrote about the company. But this is not to say that asset quality is low. In the first chart below, you can see the firm's return on assets. With a reading of 2.64%, The Bancorp is massively superior to any of the other five companies I compared it to. And it also happens to be well above the 1% minimum that I like to see in the space. In the chart below that, you can see its return on equity. The 28.40% reading that we get here is significantly greater than the 10% minimum I like to see. It's also quite a bit above any of the five firms I compared it to.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would argue that The Bancorp remains an interesting prospect. But honestly, shares have gotten a bit too expensive for my liking. I wouldn't say that the company is overvalued. But I do think it is pretty much fairly valued. Because of this, I've decided to downgrade it from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’.

data.title: Why Data Centers May Help Drive ESG-Labeled Bond Issuance
data.description:
Electricity Consumption Points to a Stronger Future for ESG-Labeled Issuance

Bond issuance linked to environmental, social and governance (ESG) purposes dipped in the first half of 2025 following a strong second half in 2024. But we expect issuance of ESG-labeled bonds will pick up for several reasons.

Many debut issuers are still coming to the market (Display, left). Borrowers from Austria, Sweden and Spain have significantly increased their ESG-labeled bond issuance, providing over 5% of global total issuance year to date. Their contributions underscore the adaptability and resilience of the ESG-labeled bond market, as many issuers continue to prioritize responsible investing.

In terms of sectors, new ESG-labeled issuance by utilities comprises a substantial part of the market, averaging around 9% of annual issuance from 2018 through 2024. We expect that growing demand for energy from data centers (Display, right) will result in higher capex by utilities and a further increase in their ESG-labeled bond issuance. Why?

First, the rollout of data centers will likely widen to include a range of industries, not just tech companies. Second, financing is likely to diversify too, with public-market debt increasingly supplementing private-market debt. Third, data centers need energy supplies that are reliable: this means not only increased power generation but also more capex to upgrade ageing electricity grids. Finally, to minimize environmental impact, utilities will need to ensure diversity of energy supplies by harnessing more renewables alongside natural gas, which we think will lend itself naturally to more ESG-labeled issuance.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Teradyne: Fair Value And Position Adjustments After This Rally
data.description:
Article Thesis

I first wrote about Teradyne, Inc. (NASDAQ:TER) in November last year, pointing to a strong economic moat in their Semiconductor Test segment, combined with a potentially higher growth in the no-moat Robotics segment. With the stock at ~$108, I was cautiously bullish, advocating a small position eventually combined with a covered Call strategy, for some extra income.

In March I was clearly bullish, with the stock at ~$87 and a Fair Value estimate of $115.7, and I added to my position, but keeping it at ~0.75x of a normal position, because that was a period with high tariff uncertainty.

After the rally to ~$133 these days, my position grew to about 1.2x due to stock appreciation, and I am a little uncomfortable with an over-sized position for a stock that exceeded Fair Value. I present below a Fair Value reassessment, my adjusted position and a plan for the near future.

Business Segments

The second quarter brought revenue above their mid-point guidance, but down YoY:

Semiconductor Test, their largest segment (~75% of revenue) dragged down the overall performance, with sales down 12% YoY. However, looking forward, I am encouraged by CEO Greg Smith's remarks at Goldman Sachs Communicopia + Technology Conference a few weeks ago, about Teradyne’s increased market share in Compute:

I think people are going to be kind of stunned by how strong Teradyne is in compute. So Teradyne has historically been very, very strong in mobile and automotive in the test space. And we've been historically much, much lower share in the compute part of the market. But over the past couple of years, we've invested significant amounts of energy in R&D, sales and marketing and applications to try to capture new customers in this space. And I think if you look back 5 years from now going backward, people are going to be like, wow, where did that come from?

To put things in context, the company aggregates the following sub-segments in Compute:

  • VIP (Vertically Integrated Platforms), with about 50% market share.
  • Networking, with a very high market share.
  • Merchant GPU, with a very low market share unfortunately, because this is the majority of the compute TAM, “probably more than 2x the VIP compute”.

Overall, Teradyne’s market share in Compute was 10%-15% in 2024, but it’s expected to gradually increase over time, with a high probability expansion in the merchant GPU space, a movement also praised by analysts.

The mobile TAM, where Teradyne is the leader, is a depressed ~$800M in 2025, compared to peaks of ~$2B, but they expect a partial recovery in 2026, because “in both the Android ecosystem and the iOS ecosystem, there is a significant inflection in compute power that's coming to phones in the late 2026 product introductions”. And I expect this recovery to gather steam in 2027 and 2028, with the development of Edge AI.

Product Test performed better in Q2, with sales up 7% YoY. Much like mobile, the HDD TAM is way down from its peak but is poised for recovery, driven by rising data generation from AI and cloud computing. SSD is a space where Teradyne had very little share, much like merchant GPU, but they think they are “positioned to grow our share”.

But maybe the most important part of this segment comes from the acquisition of Quantifi Photonics, with co-packaged optics and pluggable optics becoming a must in scale-up and scale-out data center architectures.

Robotics is also down 17% YoY, with continued weakness in automotive and industrial automation, but I like their future repositioning towards higher growth markets:

We are in the midst of trying to reposition that into more faster-growing end segments like logistics, pharma, semiconductor and electronics, and that work is ongoing….but it's not going to be until 2027 that it's a very significant part of the revenue mix.

Financial Analysis

Some of the signs of recovery mentioned in the above section are visible in Teradyne’s Q3’25 guidance:

For the whole year, consensus is for an anemic 2.6% growth, and I don’t argue with that. However, much more important is their mid-term (2028) model, because there are some segments only in an early stage for now, like merchant GPU or Quantifi Potonics, while other markets are also expected to grow more sharply in 2027, like robotics or mobile:

Switching to profitability, gross margin is seen relatively constant at 59% to 60% for the visible future. However, a significant part of OpEx is variabilized, and the company sees 4% to 6% OpEx growth for 10% top line growth, although this leverage is hard to achieve with just 5% top line growth.

Finally, let’s look at their capital allocation strategy:

Like I mentioned in my previous article when the stock was around $85, this is exactly the picture that I wanted to see for 2025, with increased buybacks at a depressed price, even more than free cash flow. For 2024 they kept their buybacks contained, with a higher stock price. This is different from the picture that we saw in 2020, with minor buybacks at a depressed price, or in 2021-2022, with large buybacks at an elevated price.

Fair Value

Like mentioned in my previous article, a comparison with Teradyne’s five-year averages for a relative valuation is not very effective due to some cyclicality, so high multiples don’t necessary mean overvaluation, but could indicate a bottom of the cycle and vice-versa.

We can look at their peer, Advantest (OTCPK:ATEYY), and we can see about the same EV/EBITDA, but significantly higher EV/Sales. However, this is normal due to Advantest’s much higher profitability.

Therefore, let’s move to a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model for an absolute valuation. I model three scenarios:

Scenario 1

(pessimistic)

Scenario 2

(base-case)

Scenario 3

(optimistic)

Revenue 2028

$4.3B

$4.75B

$5.5B

Rev. growth ‘29-‘34

-5%-10%

-5%-15%

0%-15%

Gross margin

59%-60%

59%-60%

59%-60%

OpEx growth

½ rev. growth

½ rev. growth

½ rev. growth

EBIT margin 2034

31%

34%

37%

Tax rate

18%

15%

13%

Perpetual growth

3%

3.75%

4.25%

WACC

10.1%

10.1%

10.1%

Fair Value

$90.3

$129

$195.2

For 2028 I look at their model (see Figure #3), and:

  • In the base-case scenario, I model $4.75B revenue in 2028, within their target range but higher than I modeled in March, due to their adventure in the GPU space.
  • For the optimistic scenario, I model their upper limit ($5.5B), which they are revising from time to time anyway, it was only $5B last quarter.
  • For the pessimistic scenario, I am more aligned with consensus, which is more pessimistic, so I assume they will miss even their lower limit ($4.5B).

WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital), which is 100% cost of equity, is 10.1%, and here is how I estimate it:

  • I start from 11.25% which I use for a no-moat IT hardware company in this period, and which is close to 11% seen by Aswath Damodaran for the Semiconductor Equipment industry.
  • Then I subtract 0.75 for their strong economic moat (about 75% of the business), 0.5 for their cash position, 0.25 for their profitability.
  • And I add back 0.25 for their cyclicality, and 0.1 for their ties with China.

Resulting Fair Value for the base-case scenario is $129, very close to the current price (~$133).

Position and Risk Management

As mentioned with other occasions, the weight of a position in my portfolio is given by my estimates for rating and economic moat:

Strong Buy

Buy

Hold

Strong moat

2x

1.5x

1x

Narrow moat/ETF

1.5x

1x

0.75x

No moat

1x

0.75x

0.5x

Extreme uncertainty

0.25x

…where 1x = a normal weight position.

Terdayne is between “Narrow moat” and “Strong moat”, so I think it deserves between 0.75x and 1x exposure in the "Hold" zone. Like mentioned in the introduction, my position grew to about 1.2x today due to price appreciation, so I am a little uncomfortable with that. That’s why I trimmed my position by about 25% two days ago (September 24) at $133, because the stock exceeded my Fair Value estimate, bringing it to ~0.9x.

For a slightly pessimistic scenario, I take the average between the base-case fair value ($129) and the pessimistic one ($90.3), which gives ~$109, and this is the level below which I am comfortable buying more.

For a slightly optimistic scenario, I take the average between the base-case fair value and the optimistic one ($195.2), which gives $162, and this is the level above which I am comfortable selling.

However, when the stock becomes overvalued, many times for strong companies I sell a simple covered Call option instead of selling my shares in order to collect income, because if the stock drops again to the Fair Value zone, I don’t mind keeping it after collecting healthy option premium. This is the exact strategy that I described for my position in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which is also a narrow moat stock very close to my fair value estimate.

I plan another 10%-12% price appreciation from the Call option’s strike price to my exit target of $162. That means that I could sell an OTM (Out-of-The-Money) TER 165 Call option expiring in about 6 months when Teradyne is above $148, for an estimated ~$1400 premium, depending on the IV (Implied Volatility) at that time. For example:

Yes, this will cap my profit above $165, but with my cost basis around $91.25, that means a comfortable ~113.5% ROI. Besides, it reduces my break-even to $77.25. More, I will get more income than selling my shares even at $175, because:

  • I get an extra $1700 from price appreciation from $148 to $165, since I will be assigned at $165.
  • I get an extra ~$1400 from the collected premium.

Even more, if Teradyne will be around $165 (maybe +/-15%), I can buy back my 165 Call option when it has very low extrinsic value remained, and sell another ~6 months options, to keep collecting income, but only after reassessing Fair Value, of course.

And finally, I always try to sell these Call options when IV is high, because this alone will drag up the option price, so I can collect even more income.

Takeaway

Teradyne's Semiconductor Test segment experienced YoY revenue declines in Q2'25 due to some cyclicality, but shows long-term promise, especially with potential GPU market share gains, which is their largest TAM. Product Test performed relatively well, and is expected to accelerate with the acquisition of Quantifi Photonics. Robotics faces short-term headwinds in automotive, but is being restructured towards higher growth markets.

Base-case Fair Value estimate, derived from a 10-year DCF model, is ~$129, with current price at ~$133. After a strong rally from previous lows, my position grew to ~1.2x, and I trimmed it to ~0.9x, consistent with a narrow-to-strong moat company in the Hold zone.

I am comfortable buying more under $109, and selling above $162. However, instead of selling my shares I usually target a covered Call exit strategy, by selling a 10%-12% OTM Call option with Teradyne above $148. In this way, I reduce my break-even and I don't mind keeping the position if it drops again in Fair Value zone, while collecting healthy income from rolling the Call option with Teradyne around $165 (+/-15%).

data.title: It's Shutdown Time Again
data.description:
Stop us if you’ve heard this one before. The US government shuts down for some defined span of time, markets barely notice, and for everyone who doesn’t work for the federal government, life goes on more or less as normal. Well, there might be some disgruntled folks at closed national parks, if it gets that far. But that’s about it.

So it looks like the government will be shutting down again next week, on September 30, unless some completely unexpected series of talks emerge between the parties, where currently none exist. So, more of the same, right? Or is this time different, and markets should be paying more attention?

Dysfunctional In Their Own Unique Ways

Government shutdowns are like the unhappy families of Tolstoy’s opening line in “Anna Karenina” – each one is dysfunctional in its own special way. The government has actually shut down a number of times over the previous few decades, of which three stand out in particular.

In November 1995, then-President Clinton refused to sign off on a slew of budget cuts demanded by Newt Gingrich’s Republicans, the latter of whom also threatened to refuse to raise the debt ceiling limit (familiar, no?).

The shutdown – actually two shutdowns with a brief interlude between them – lasted from November 14, 1994 to January 6, 1996. During that time the S&P 500 gained 4.7 percent amid an otherwise perky period of economic growth.

During the administration of Barack Obama, one of the most perennially contentious issues was the Affordable Care Act, which galvanized Republican opposition like nothing else. In October 2013, the ACA was the key issue in a failure by Congress to either appropriate funds for the following fiscal year or to rig up a continuing resolution for interim funding.

A shutdown ensued, lasting for seventeen days, during which all but the most critical government functions were curtailed, 800,000 federal workers were furloughed and another 1.3 million continued to work in some form without knowing when they would next be paid. The shutdown generated major daily news headlines during this period. Again, though, the stock market barely paid attention. The S&P 500 rose 1.6 percent from October 1 to October 17.

The longest shutdown to date lasted for 35 days, from December 22, 2018 to January 25, 2019. The Congressional Budget Office estimated that this prolonged shutdown cost the US economy $11 billion, as about a third of all government operations were completely non-functional for this period.

This time, the main point of dispute was disagreement over $5.7 billion in funding for Trump’s border wall between the US and Mexico. After a great amount of fudging and performative Kabuki drama on all sides, the shutdown fizzled out and life went on.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 actually rose 10.5 percent over this 35-day period (the stock market’s movements during this time actually had almost nothing to do with the shutdown and everything to do with the Fed, near the end of a period of monetary tightening).

Health Care, Rescissions and Mass Firings

So what can we expect next week, when the government appears poised to shut down again? Well, the issues on the table would appear to be intractable. Messaging on the Democratic side appears to have settled around the issue of health insurance subsidies, which are due to expire, with House Minority Leader Jeffries insisting on a measure to protect these subsidies and related health care benefits in the form of “ironclad” legislation.

More generally, the Democrats are trying to figure out how to stop a repeat of the last budget go-around earlier this year, when they agreed to a continuation in funding only to have some elements of that funding yanked away by the Republicans in the Recissions Act of 2025 (recission being the technical term for “we said you could have this, but we’re taking it away now, and you can’t stop us”).

None of this appears to be ruffling any feathers on the Republican side, and in fact the White House has threatened to use the shutdown as an excuse to implement mass federal firings throughout the swath of government agencies.

The strategic logic on the Republican side appears to be that the pain caused by such broad-based evisceration of agency personnel will be too much for the Democrats (who, they assume, would bear the brunt of the blame), causing them to fold. A veritable game of chicken is at hand.

The markets, as usual, are not paying attention to much or any of this. History would seem to be on their side: a government shutdown becomes a real, economically consequential event if it goes on indefinitely, but eventually something will give and some messy compromise will get the funds flowing again. That’s how it has always worked, and the S&P 500 has the receipts.

The problem this time is that the general creditability of the US system is not exactly riding high these days, either here or elsewhere in the world among dollar-denominated asset holders.

We don’t expect this looming shutdown to last indefinitely or even that it will break that prior record of 35 days. But the dysfunction is just one more indication that things are not working as they should, and this concern will be very much on our minds as we start to plan our allocation strategy for 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
